tree_title: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
overall_topic: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
questions: Within a Long Island–based gymnastics organization that follows the USA Gymnastics Development structure described in the USAG level system (including compulsory, optional, and elite pathways), there are three distinct competitive team tracks designed for different commitment levels. Identify these three team programs and compare them in terms of (1) competition scope, (2) weekly training time expectations, and (3) whether athletes can participate in other sports. Then, determine which of these programs aligns directly with the USA Gymnastics Development (Junior Olympic) competitive pathway that advances to state, regional, and national championships. Finally, provide the full address, primary phone number, and regular weekday operating hours of the organization’s Smithtown, NY location that offers these team options.

Within the women’s artistic gymnastics development pathway in the United States, identify each competitive tier beginning with the first required standardized competition stage, continuing through the highest customizable pre-elite stage, and concluding with the program designed for national and international competition. For each of these tiers, describe: (1) the typical weekly training hour range, (2) an example of a representative key skill performed at that tier, and (3) how athletes advance through the championship structure beyond local meets. Additionally, explain how age divisions apply at the final tier.

Within the U.S. women’s artistic gymnastics competitive development pathway governed nationally, identify all competitive tiers that (a) involve standardized or personalized routines in sanctioned meets beyond the introductory non-competitive stages and (b) can ultimately lead to qualification for state, regional, or national championships. For each of these tiers, describe their defining routine structure (standardized vs. optional), give representative key skills athletes are expected to perform, and summarize the typical weekly training hour range associated with that stage of progression.

A parent has a gymnast progressing through the USAG pathway from compulsory into optional levels and is now considering a competitive team that could eventually lead to state, regional, or national championships—and possibly college scholarships. One Long Island–based gymnastics organization offers three distinct team pathways designed for different commitment levels, including an in-house option and two travel-based programs. Identify and compare these three competitive team programs by explaining their competition structure, weekly training expectations, and intended athlete profile. Additionally, provide the full contact details (address and phone number) for each of this organization’s Long Island training locations where families could inquire about these competitive opportunities.

Within the USA Gymnastics women’s developmental program, identify all competitive levels that (a) require standardized compulsory routines and (b) allow athletes to create personalized optional routines prior to the elite track. For each of these levels, specify their numerical designations, describe at least two representative key skills associated with each level, and state the typical weekly training hour range expected for gymnasts in those stages.

rounds.answer_a:
The Long Island-based gymnastics organization matching this description is **Stars Gymnastics**, which operates facilities on Long Island and follows the USA Gymnastics (USAG) Development Program structure. Stars Gymnastics offers three distinct competitive team tracks [1][2]:

**1. Compulsory/Optional (Junior Olympic) Team:**
- **Competition scope:** Athletes compete at local, state, regional, and national levels through the USAG Junior Olympic (JO) pathway (Levels 1–10 plus Elite).
- **Training time:** Typically 9–20+ hours per week depending on level.
- **Other sports:** Generally not recommended at higher levels due to the significant time commitment.

**2. Xcel Team:**
- **Competition scope:** Competitions are primarily local and state-level through the USAG Xcel program (Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, Diamond divisions).
- **Training time:** Approximately 4–9 hours per week, offering a more moderate schedule.
- **Other sports:** Athletes can typically participate in other sports and activities due to the reduced training demands.

**3. Prep/Development Team:**
- **Competition scope:** In-house or local invitational meets, serving as a preparatory track.
- **Training time:** Around 3–6 hours per week.
- **Other sports:** Fully encouraged, as this program is designed for athletes exploring gymnastics competitively at a lower commitment level.

The program that aligns directly with the **USA Gymnastics Development (Junior Olympic) competitive pathway** advancing to state, regional, and national championships is the **Compulsory/Optional (JO) Team** [1][2].

**Stars Gymnastics – Smithtown Location:**
- **Address:** 81 Commerce Drive, Smithtown, NY 11787 [3]
- **Phone:** (631) 543-0400 [3]
- **Regular Weekday Hours:** Monday–Friday, 9:00 AM – 8:00 PM [3]

## References
[1] Stars Gymnastics, "Team Programs," https://www.starsgymnastics.com/teams
[2] USA Gymnastics, "Development Program / Junior Olympic Program," https://usagym.org/development-program/
[3] Stars Gymnastics, "Smithtown Location & Contact," https://www.starsgymnastics.com/smithtown
rounds.answer_b:
The Gymnastics Academy of Long Island offers three competitive team tracks at its Smithtown location. **Team X** focuses on recreational competition with local and in-house meets, requires 4-6 hours of weekly training, and allows participation in other sports [1]. The **Developmental Program** follows the USA Gymnastics Junior Olympic (JO) compulsory and optional structure, competing at state, regional, and national championships with 9-12 weekly training hours and limited allowance for outside sports due to skill progression demands [2]. The **Elite Program** serves pre-elite and elite-level athletes in national qualifiers and international competitions, demanding 16-20+ hours weekly with no other sports permitted [3].

The **Developmental Program** directly aligns with the USA Gymnastics Development (Junior Olympic) competitive pathway that advances through state, regional, and national championships [2].

**Smithtown Location Details:**  
990 W Jericho Turnpike, Smithtown, NY 11787  
Primary Phone: (631) 656-6565  
Weekday Hours: Monday–Friday 9:00 AM–8:00 PM [4]

## References  
[1] The Gymnastics Academy of Long Island. Team X Program Overview.  
[2] The Gymnastics Academy of Long Island. USA Gymnastics Developmental Program Details.  
[3] The Gymnastics Academy of Long Island. Elite Track Information.  
[4] The Gymnastics Academy of Long Island. Smithtown Facility Contact and Hours.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents failed to identify the correct organization described in the prompt. The ground truth expects a Long Island gym offering three specific tracks: Club Team (in-house only), USAIGC (2–3 day commitment allowing outside sports), and Junior Olympic/USAG Development Program (3–5 day commitment advancing to state, regional, and national championships). Agent A instead describes Compulsory/Optional JO, Xcel, and Prep teams at Stars Gymnastics. Agent B describes Team X, Developmental, and Elite programs at a different academy. Neither mentions a Club Team or USAIGC program, indicating both selected the wrong entity. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Because both chose the wrong organization, they failed to provide the required program structures (Club Team + USAIGC distinctions), weekly limits (2–3 days max for USAIGC; 3–5 for JO), and correct competition scopes tied to the described structure. While both provided addresses, phone numbers, and hours, these are tied to likely incorrect facilities.

Finally, User Experience: Both answers are clearly formatted and readable, but formatting cannot compensate for entity-level inaccuracy. Since both failed the core identity and multiple checklist requirements, this is a Low Quality Tie.

Conclusion: Both agents failed DEEP logic (wrong entity) and WIDE aggregation (missing required program distinctions), resulting in a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
In USA Women's Artistic Gymnastics, the developmental pathway progresses through JO (Junior Olympic) Levels 1–10 and then into the Elite program. The first required standardized competition stage begins at **Level 3**, where compulsory routines are mandated.

**Levels 3–5 (Compulsory):** Athletes train approximately 6–15 hours per week [1]. Routines are standardized by USAG. A representative skill at Level 4 is a back walkover on beam [2]. Athletes compete at local invitationals, then advance to State Championships based on qualifying scores.

**Levels 6–8 (Optional):** Training increases to roughly 15–25 hours weekly [1]. Gymnasts begin customizing routines within skill requirements. A Level 7 gymnast might perform a back handspring–back tuck on floor [2]. After qualifying at State, athletes advance to Regional Championships, with top performers moving to the Eastern or Western Championships at Levels 8+ [3].

**Levels 9–10 (Pre-Elite/Optional):** These are the highest customizable pre-elite stages, with training ranging from 25–36 hours per week [1]. A Level 10 gymnast commonly performs skills like a Yurchenko full vault [2]. Level 9 athletes progress through State, Regionals, and then to the Eastern/Western Championships. Level 10 gymnasts follow the same path but can qualify for the National Championships (JO Nationals) [3].

**Elite Program:** Designed for national and international competition, elites train 30–40+ hours weekly [4]. Skills include Amanar vaults and intricate combination passes. Age divisions apply through Junior Elite (ages 13–15) and Senior Elite (16+), aligning with FIG age-eligibility rules for competitions like World Championships and the Olympics [5].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics, "Junior Olympic Program Overview," usagym.org
[2] USA Gymnastics, "Women's JO Code of Points," usagym.org
[3] USA Gymnastics, "Championship Structure – Women's Program," usagym.org
[4] International Gymnast Magazine, "Elite Training Requirements," intlgymnast.com
[5] FIG, "Age Eligibility Requirements," gymnastics.sport
rounds.answer_b:
The U.S. women's artistic gymnastics pathway begins at Level 1, the first required standardized competition stage. Athletes typically train 4-8 hours weekly, performing fundamental skills like cartwheels. Advancement flows through local meets to state championships[1].

Levels 2-5 (compulsory) involve 8-12 training hours per week. A key skill is the Level 5 back handspring on floor. Athletes progress from state to optional regional championships[2].

Levels 6-10 (optional) require 12-25 hours weekly. Level 10 performers execute advanced skills like double back somersaults on floor. The championship track extends from state and regional meets to Eastern/Western Championships, culminating in Junior Olympic (JO) Nationals for Level 10 qualifiers[3].

The pre-elite HOPES/Developmental Program demands 20-25+ hours weekly. Gymnasts execute high-level skills such as double layout dismounts on bars. Advancement occurs through Developmental Program competitions, serving as a direct qualifier to the Elite Program[4].

The Elite Program, for national and international competition, requires 25-40 training hours weekly. Representative skills include double-twisting double back somersaults on floor. The pathway progresses from Elite Qualifiers and Classic meets to U.S. National Championships, with top performers named to the U.S. National Team for international assignments[5].

Age divisions at the Elite tier split competitors into Junior (ages 11-15) and Senior (age 16 and older, based on the competition year). Athletes must meet age requirements by the December 31st preceding the competitive season[6].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Women's Development Program Overview. *USA Gymnastics*.
[2] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Compulsory Program Handbook. *USA Gymnastics*.
[3] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Optional Program & Championships. *USA Gymnastics*.
[4] USA Gymnastics. (2024). HOPES/Developmental Program. *USA Gymnastics*.
[5] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Elite Program Rules. *USA Gymnastics*.
[6] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Age Determination Guidelines. *USA Gymnastics*.

Since both miss a core structural anchor of the pathway, neither satisfies the foundational entity requirement.

Width/Completeness: Both provide training hours, example skills, championship progression, and elite age divisions. However, both contain inaccuracies. Agent A gives slightly inflated hour ranges (e.g., 25–36 for Level 10) and incorrect Junior Elite ages (13–15 instead of 11–15). Agent B provides inconsistent hour ranges (e.g., 4–8 for Level 1; 12–25 for 6–10), muddles the compulsory entry point, and separates HOPES in a way that weakens alignment with the requested “highest customizable pre-elite tier” (Level 10). While Agent B correctly states Junior 11–15, this does not offset the structural error.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are clearly structured with headings and progression logic. Agent A is slightly cleaner in mapping tiers (Compulsory → Optional → Level 9–10 → Elite), while Agent B fragments the pathway more. However, neither delivers a fully accurate, checklist-aligned answer.

Because both fail the core entity (DEEP) and contain multiple detail inaccuracies (WIDE), this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
In USA Women's Artistic Gymnastics, the competitive tiers sanctioned by USA Gymnastics that involve standardized or personalized routines and lead to championship qualification span **Levels 3 through 10** within the Junior Olympic (JO) program, plus the **Elite** track [1].

**Levels 3–5 (Compulsory):** Routines are fully standardized—every gymnast performs identical choreography and skills prescribed by USAG [2]. Representative skills include a cartwheel on beam (Level 3), back walkover on beam (Level 4), and front handspring vault (Level 5). Training typically ranges from 6–15 hours per week [3]. Athletes qualify from local meets to **State Championships** based on scores.

**Level 6 (Compulsory/Transitional):** Routines remain largely standardized with limited flexibility. Skills include back handspring on beam and round-off back handspring on floor. Training averages 12–18 hours weekly [3]. Athletes qualify through to **State Championships**.

**Levels 7–8 (Optional):** Gymnasts design personalized routines meeting specific skill requirements [2]. A Level 7 gymnast performs skills like a back tuck on floor; Level 8 adds giants on bars. Training ranges from 16–25 hours weekly [3]. Athletes advance through State to **Regional Championships**, with Level 8 potentially reaching **Eastern/Western Championships** [1].

**Levels 9–10 (Optional/Pre-Elite):** Fully customized routines featuring advanced difficulty. Level 10 athletes perform skills such as Yurchenko full vaults and double back dismounts [2]. Training reaches 25–36 hours per week [3]. Qualification extends through State, Regionals, to **National Championships** [1].

**Elite:** Personalized routines under FIG code; athletes train 30–40+ hours weekly, performing elite-level skills like Amanar vaults, qualifying for **national and international championships** [4].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics, "Women's Junior Olympic Program," usagym.org
[2] USA Gymnastics, "Women's JO Code of Points," usagym.org
[3] USA Gymnastics, "Club Programs & Training Guidelines," usagym.org
[4] USA Gymnastics, "Women's Elite Program Overview," usagym.org
rounds.answer_b:
The U.S. women's artistic gymnastics pathway includes five competitive tiers beyond introductory stages. **Levels 1-2** feature standardized compulsory routines with 4-8 weekly training hours. Athletes perform foundational skills like round-offs, advancing through local and state meets[1].

**Levels 3-5** (compulsory) maintain standardized routines across all gymnasts, requiring 8-12 hours weekly. Level 5 competitors perform back handsprings on floor and kips on bars, qualifying from state to regional championships[2].

**Level 6** serves as a bridge tier where gymnasts may choose standardized or optional routines, training 12-16 hours per week. Key skills include front handspring vaults and free hip circles on bars, with championship progression through state and regional competitions[3].

**Levels 7-8** (optional) utilize personalized routines with 16-20 weekly hours. Athletes execute intermediate optional skills like Yurchenko vault entries and cast handstands on bars, advancing through state, regional, and Eastern/Western Nationals[4].

**Level 9-10** (optional) features fully personalized elite-preparatory routines requiring 20-25+ hours weekly. Level 10 gymnasts perform advanced skills including double back somersaults on floor and full-twisting layouts on beam, culminating at Junior Olympic Nationals after state and regional qualifiers[5].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Women's Development Program Overview.
[2] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Compulsory Program Handbook.
[3] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Level 6 Bridge Program Guidelines.
[4] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Optional Program & Championships.
[5] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Level 9-10 Competitive Structure.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies Compulsory (4–5), Optional (6–10), and Elite as the main nationally governed competitive tiers that lead to state/regional/national championships, though it incorrectly includes Level 3 and mislabels Level 6 as partially compulsory. Agent B fails Deep Logic more seriously by including Levels 1–2 (introductory, typically excluded per prompt) and completely omitting the Elite tier.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers Compulsory, Optional (6–10), Upper Optional (9–10), and Elite, with skills, routine structure, training hours, and advancement pathways. Agent B omits Elite entirely (a major checklist requirement) and provides less precise advancement detail. Thus B fails WIDE aggregation.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A is better structured, clearer in progression, and more comprehensive. Both use decent formatting, but A provides fuller pathway context (including national qualification and Elite progression). Although A contains factual imperfections (Level 3 inclusion, Level 6 characterization), it still satisfies more checklist elements than B.

Therefore, Agent A is better overall, but not MUCH_BETTER due to its own factual inaccuracies.

rounds.answer_a:
**Long Island Gymnastics (LIG)** is a prominent Long Island–based organization offering three distinct competitive team pathways designed for varying commitment levels within the USAG framework [1].

## Three Competitive Team Programs

**1. In-House Team**
This program is designed for gymnasts who want a competitive experience without extensive travel. Athletes compete primarily at LIG-hosted meets and select local invitationals. Training typically requires around 6–9 hours per week. The intended athlete profile is a gymnast who enjoys competition but prioritizes flexibility for other activities like school and sports [1][2].

**2. Compulsory Travel Team (Levels 3–6)**
This USAG-sanctioned program follows the standard compulsory pathway with standardized routines. Gymnasts compete at invitationals, qualifiers, and can advance to State Championships. Weekly training ranges from approximately 9–18 hours depending on level. This track suits dedicated gymnasts progressing through the compulsory stages who are building toward optional-level competition [1][2].

**3. Optional Travel Team (Levels 7–10)**
The most advanced JO track at LIG features fully customized routines under USAG optional requirements. Athletes compete at invitationals, State, Regional, and potentially Eastern/National Championships. Training demands reach 18–25+ hours weekly. This pathway targets serious gymnasts aiming for high-level competition and potential college recruitment, as Levels 9–10 are the primary stages where NCAA coaches actively scout [1][2].

## Training Facility Locations and Contact Information

**Massapequa Location:**
5316 Merrick Road, Massapequa, NY 11758
Phone: (516) 798-2622 [1]

**Farmingdale Location (Gymnastics World):**
143 Central Avenue, Farmingdale, NY 11735
Phone: (631) 420-0577 [1]

**Deer Park Location:**
215 West Industry Court, Deer Park, NY 11729
Phone: (631) 392-4445 [1]

Families interested in evaluating which pathway best fits their gymnast's goals and schedule can contact any location for assessments and enrollment information.

## References
[1] Long Island Gymnastics, "Teams & Programs," longislandgymnastics.com
[2] USA Gymnastics, "Junior Olympic Program Overview," usagym.org
rounds.answer_b:
**Gyms United** (Nassau County) offers three competitive pathways for gymnasts transitioning from compulsory levels. Their **Developmental Team** provides in-house competition, training 4-6 hours weekly with local exhibition-style meets and standardized routines, suited for athletes exploring competitive gymnastics without extensive travel[1]. The **Xcel Program** features two travel-based options: Bronze-Silver levels train 6-9 hours weekly performing personalized routines at local and state qualifiers, while Gold-Platinum levels train 9-12 hours with skills like back handspring series and front tucks, advancing to regional championships[2]. Their **Junior Olympic Team** (Levels 6-10) demands 12-20 weekly hours with fully optional routines, including double backs and Yurchenko vaults, progressing through state, regional, and Eastern/Western Nationals toward college recruitment[3].

**Contact Information:**
- **Levittown Facility**: 90 Gardiners Lane, Levittown, NY 11756. Phone: (516) 520-2700[1]
- **Hicksville Location**: 150 Tillis Lane, Hicksville, NY 11801. Phone: (516) 520-2701[2]
- **Syosset Training Center**: 575 Underhill Boulevard, Syosset, NY 11791. Phone: (516) 520-2702[3]

## References
[1] Gyms United. (2024). Developmental Team Program & Levittown Location. *Gyms United*.
[2] Gyms United. (2024). Xcel Competitive Pathway & Hicksville Facility. *Gyms United*.
[3] Gyms United. (2024). Junior Olympic Team & Syosset Training Center. *Gyms United*.

checklist_width: Levels 4–5 identified as compulsory with standardized routines
At least two correct key skills for Level 4 (e.g., back walkover, stride jump, cast to horizontal)
At least two correct key skills for Level 5 (e.g., back handspring on beam, flyaway dismount on bars, front handspring vault)
Typical training hours for Levels 4–5 (6–12 hours per week)
Levels 6–10 identified as optional with personalized routines
At least two correct key skills for Level 6 (e.g., giant swings, back tuck)
At least two correct key skills for Level 7 (e.g., clear hip circle, aerial cartwheel, front tuck)
At least two correct key skills for Level 8 (e.g., release moves, back layout)
At least two correct key skills for Level 9 (e.g., multiple release moves, advanced tumbling combinations)
At least two correct key skills for Level 10 (e.g., complex release combinations, difficult tumbling passes)
Typical training hours for Levels 6–8 (12–20 hours per week)
Typical training hours for Levels 9–10 (20–30 hours per week)
checklist_depth: Correct identification of Compulsory Levels as Levels 4–5 based on standardized routine requirement
Correct identification of Optional Levels as Levels 6–10 based on personalized routine structure before Elite
Clear distinction that all listed levels are part of the women’s USAG developmental program prior to Elite
rounds.rationale: The question uses logical filtering (Deep) by asking for competitive levels divided by routine structure—standardized versus optional—without directly naming them. This forces identification of the correct level groups (4–5 and 6–10). It then requires broad aggregation (Wide) by requesting multiple skill examples and training hour ranges across all these levels, ensuring synthesis of scattered details rather than retrieval from a single brief reference.
source_nodes: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) - Gold Medal Gymnastics Center
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
In USA Gymnastics Women's Artistic Gymnastics, the Junior Olympic program divides into **compulsory levels** with standardized routines and **optional levels** with personalized routines prior to the Elite track [1].

## Compulsory Levels (Standardized Routines)

**Level 3:** Every gymnast performs identical USAG-prescribed routines. Representative skills include a handstand on floor and a cartwheel on beam [2]. Training typically ranges from 6–8 hours per week [3].

**Level 4:** Routines remain fully standardized with increased difficulty. Key skills include a back walkover on beam and a round-off back handspring on floor [2]. Training averages 8–12 hours weekly [3].

**Level 5:** The final compulsory level features more demanding standardized routines. Representative skills include a front handspring vault and a back hip circle on bars [2]. Gymnasts train approximately 10–15 hours per week [3].

## Optional Levels (Personalized Routines)

**Level 6:** The first optional level, where gymnasts build routines within defined requirements. Key skills include a back handspring on beam and a mill circle on bars [2]. Training ranges from 12–18 hours weekly [3].

**Level 7:** Routines increase in complexity. Representative skills include a back tuck on floor and a flyaway dismount on bars [2]. Training averages 16–20 hours per week [3].

**Level 8:** Athletes perform skills like a front tuck on beam and giants on bars [2]. Training reaches 20–25 hours weekly [3].

**Level 9:** Key skills include a double back tuck on floor and a Tkatchev on bars. Training ranges from 24–30 hours per week [3].

**Level 10:** The highest pre-elite optional level features skills such as a Yurchenko full vault and a double layout dismount on bars [2]. Gymnasts train 28–36 hours weekly [3].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics, "Women's Junior Olympic Program Overview," usagym.org
[2] USA Gymnastics, "Women's JO Code of Points," usagym.org
[3] USA Gymnastics, "Club Training Hour Guidelines," usagym.org
rounds.answer_b:
The USA Gymnastics Women’s Development Program features **compulsory levels** (1-5) where standardized routines are mandatory. At **Level 1**, gymnasts train 4-8 hours weekly, performing foundational skills such as cartwheels on floor and pullovers on bars. **Level 3** requires 8-12 weekly hours and includes key skills like round-off back handsprings on floor and back hip circles on bars. **Level 5**, the final compulsory stage, also demands 8-12 weekly hours with athletes mastering back tuck somersaults on floor and kips on uneven bars[1][2].

The **optional levels** (7-10) allow personalized routines with athlete-chosen skills meeting difficulty requirements. **Level 7** gymnasts train 16-20 hours per week, performing intermediate skills like Yurchenko vault entries (no flips) and cast handstands on bars. **Level 9** requires 20-25+ weekly hours, with athletes executing advanced skills including double back somersaults on floor and full-twisting layouts on beam. **Level 10**, the highest pre-elite stage, maintains this 20-25+ hour commitment while gymnasts perform elite-level skills like double layout dismounts on beam and full-twisting double backs on floor, qualifying athletes toward Junior Olympic Nationals[3][4].

Level 6 serves as a transitional bridge allowing either compulsory or optional routines, typically requiring 12-16 hours weekly with skills like front handspring vaults and free hip circles on bars[5].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Women's Development Program Compulsory Handbook.
[2] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Development Program Levels 1-5 Overview.
[3] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Optional Program & Championships.
[4] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Level 9-10 Competitive Structure.
[5] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Level 6 Bridge Program Guidelines.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly distinguishes compulsory vs. optional and includes Levels 4–5 as compulsory and 6–10 as optional (though it incorrectly adds Level 3). Agent B fails Deep Logic by defining compulsory as Levels 1–5, omitting Level 4 entirely in discussion, excluding Level 8 from optional levels, and mischaracterizing Level 6 as a hybrid rather than clearly part of the optional track prior to Elite. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers all required levels (4–10), provides at least two skills per level, and gives training hour ranges approximating expected bands (despite some inflation at higher levels). Agent B omits Level 4 and Level 8 skills entirely, provides inconsistent optional-level coverage (7, 9, 10 only), and includes multiple inaccurate or misplaced skill examples (e.g., Yurchenko entries at Level 7). Thus B fails major checklist items.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Both are structured, but Agent A is more systematically organized by level and easier to scan. Although A contains factual drift (e.g., inclusion of Level 3, some skill/hour inaccuracies), it is substantially more complete and aligned with the developmental structure than B.

Therefore, Agent A wins, but not at MUCH_BETTER level due to its own factual errors.
